articles.title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 21 April 2026
articles.description: Countries: World, Australia, Cook Islands, French Polynesia (France), Guam, Kiribati, Micronesia (Federated States of), New Caledonia (France), New Zealand, Palau, Tonga, United States of America, Vanuatu Source: Pacific Community Please refer to the attached Map. Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 14 April 2026: Dengue New Caledonia: As of 17 April 2026, dengue transmission in New Caledonia remains ongoing, with 944 cumulative cases reported since 1 January 2026, reflecting a continued increase in notifications since February and sustaining the epidemic phase. DENV-1 remains the predominant circulating serotype, and to date there have been limited hospitalisations reported, no severe cases and no intensive care admissions, with cases largely affecting young adults. The red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect. – Sources: Alerte DASS Dengue (09/04/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 13 April 2026 & La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de Nouvelle-Calédonie accessed on 21 April 2026. Samoa: As of 12 April 2026, Samoa continues to experience a prolonged dengue outbreak. Since 1 January 2025, 18,202 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,330 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported. During EpiWeek 15 (6–12 April 2026), 21 laboratory-confirmed cases were reported, representing a further decrease compared with the previous week. Nine new hospital admissions were recorded, with no ICU admissions or dengue-related deaths. DENV-1 (57%) and DENV-2 (43%) continue to co-circulate. Transmission remains widespread across Upolu (64%) and Savai‘i (36%), and children under 15 years account for 74% of reported cases. The cumulative number of dengue-related deaths remains nine. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect . – Source: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report in Samoa Issue No 52 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 16 April 2026. Tonga: On 17 April 2026, the Ministry of Health declared a dengue outbreak following an increase in confirmed and suspected cases and evidence of community transmission across Tongatapu, Ha‘apai and ‘Eua. As of 20 April 2026, 24 laboratory‐confirmed cases have been reported, including four new cases since 19 April. Tongatapu and Ha‘apai report the highest number of cases, with adolescents and young adults most affected. Two cases remain hospitalised, no deaths have been reported, and laboratory investigations are ongoing to confirm the circulating virus type. A red alert for Dengue is added . – Sources: Declaration of Dengue Fever Outbreak for Tonga - Ministry of Health Tonga official Facebook page and Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report #3 (20 April 2026) - Ministry of Health Tonga official Facebook page accessed on 21 April 2026. Measles Australia: Measles activity continues in Australia, with transmission largely associated with imported cases and secondary spread. As of mid‐April 2026, more than 80 cases have been reported nationally. In New South Wales, a new confirmed case linked to overseas travel in South‐East Asia was reported on 17 April 2026, prompting multiple public exposure alerts across Sydney and northern Sydney, including at health‐care settings and public venues. Most cases continue to occur among under‐vaccinated or vaccination‐status‐unknown individuals, with exposure often linked to international travel and household or community contacts. The red alert for measles in Australia remains in effect. – Sources: Measles alert for Sydney and northern Sydney and National Communicable Diseases Surveillance Dashboard accessed on 21 April 2026. Pertussis/Whooping cough New Zealand: Pertussis activity in New Zealand shows a continued gradual decline in weekly notifications, although transmission remains ongoing nationally. As of the week ending 10 April 2026, eight cases were reported, down from 22 cases the previous week, bringing the total reported cases for 2026 to 545. The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Source: Pertussis dashboard accessed on 21 April 2026. Other Information: Dengue New Zealand: In New Zealand (NZ), during EpiWeek 15 (11 – 17 April 2026), eight confirmed imported dengue cases and two cases under investigation were reported. Among confirmed cases, 62.5% had returned from Samoa, 25% from the Cook Islands and 12.5% from Malaysia. Given the known serological cross‐reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. – Source: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (15: 11/04/2026–17/04/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 20 April 2026. Leptospirosis French Polynesia: In French Polynesia, leptospirosis activity remains elevated during the current rainy season, with reinforced vigilance in place. During EpiWeek 15 (06–12 April 2026), seven new cases were reported (bringing the total number of cases to 67 since January 2026), including three admissions to intensive care and one death, bringing the total number of deaths in 2026 to three. Recent cases are reported mainly from Raiatea (Leeward islands) and are frequently linked to agricultural activities. Health authorities continue to emphasise early clinical recognition, prompt antibiotic treatment, and preventive measures to reduce the risk of severe disease. – Source: Bulletin de surveillance sanitaire de Polynésie française n°14/2026 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 21 April 2026.

articles.title: Cebu City enforces open burning ban amid unsafe air quality
articles.description: CEBU CITY, Philippines — As air quality across Metro Cebu continues to worsen in recent days, the Cebu City Government has ordered strict enforcement against open burning and directed environmental officers to intensify monitoring. This followed reports of unsafe air quality in the metro, driven by rising pollution from local emissions, recent fires, and weather patterns that trap pollutants in the atmosphere. Mayor Nestor Archival reminded the public that burning garbage is prohibited under national and local laws, as it contributes to air pollution and poses fire hazards. articles.title: Country diary: The most magical of frost-coated mornings | Mark Cocker
articles.description: Knotbury, Staffordshire: A truly special dawn, where last night’s ice lingered on everything, and I was joined by no fewer than six ring ouzels As I drove to this tiny moorland hamlet, the dawn sky looked so grey that I imagined it must have 100% cloud cover. Actually, there was none, and as the blue slowly crept in overhead, I could see that frost was everywhere. I also realised that there was no breeze and every sound seemed distilled, so I stopped by the first farm to record my blackbird. He has mastered the sweetest imitations of displaying golden plovers, but this was my first chance to capture them. And there he was, doing his plover notes, but throwing in snippets of curlew as extras, and when he stood in profile at the roof apex, singing, bill wide, throat feathers spiked against the heavens, I knew the morning would be magical. Continue reading...

Booking equivalent train tickets is “difficult or impossible” on almost half of the EU’s busiest international air routes, analysis from the Transport & Environment (T&E) thinktank shows. Continue reading...

articles.title: On the trail with the hunters who believe shooting big game can save Africa’s wildlife
articles.description: One way to pay for wildlife conservation is to allow the rich to bag a few animals for high prices. But critics see this approach as an exercise in neocolonialism You can kill almost anything if you’re willing to pay. Big or small. Land, water or air. Ten a penny or one of the last of its kind. There’s nearly always a way, though it might not make you popular. The Niassa special reserve, a vast reservation larger than Switzerland, stretches for 190 miles along the northern rim of Mozambique, taking in 4.2m hectares of woodland and rivers. The reserve, one of the world’s largest protected areas, is home to elephants, leopards, hyenas, zebras and about 1,000 wild lions. That word, however: protected. It applies to some, but not all, of its animal inhabitants. Each year, a specific number are set aside for sacrifice, for the greater good. Not long ago, I joined an expedition in Niassa, with one of Africa’s top game-hunting companies. Continue reading...

articles.title: HEAT INDEX ALERT: Cavite expected to reach ‘danger’ level Tuesday
articles.description: MANILA, Philippines — The highest heat index nationwide may reach 43°C in Sangley Point, Cavite, placing it under the “danger” category, while Metro Manila is expected to remain under the “extreme caution” level with temperatures ranging from 38°C to 39°C, according to the state weather bureau. Heat index refers to the “feels like” temperature, or how hot it actually feels to the human body when air temperature is combined with humidity, the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) said.
